This requires all the images to have the same size.  There is also an argument of the network that determines the mini-batch size.  For instance, if dimgs contained a million images, it wouldn't try to stuff 1 million images onto the GPU, it would be chopped up into batches of 128 (by default) and run.  The 128 value is the optional 2nd argument to net() and should also be an optional argument here, otherwise it will be impossible to run this function with large images since you will run out of GPU ram, but setting the batch size to something smaller would work fine. 

So this function needs two things.  A way to set the batch size, and a check that the user gave images that are all of the same size.  This check that all images are the same size is performed internally in the network as well, but the error message from there will probably confuse python users.

Yes this is accurate! I was hesitant to use `addLevelName` because for Python 2 it will replace the numeric portion of the dictionary value with our custom level. For example, here is what happens with Python 3:

```python
In [3]: logging._nameToLevel
Out[3]: 
{'CRITICAL': 50,
 'DEBUG': 10,
 'ERROR': 40,
 'INFO': 20,
 'NOTSET': 0,
 'WARN': 30,
 'WARNING': 30}

In [4]: logging.addLevelName(logging.DEBUG,'HAKUNAMATATA')

In [5]: logging._nameToLevel
Out[5]: 
{'CRITICAL': 50,
 'DEBUG': 10,
 'ERROR': 40,
 'HAKUNAMATATA': 10,
 'INFO': 20,
 'NOTSET': 0,
 'WARN': 30,
 'WARNING': 30}
```
Level "DEBUG" and "HAKUNAMATAKA" are both maintained with level 10. But for Python 2, the portion of the dictionary with the 10 as index is overwritten by our update:

```python
In [2]: logging._levelNames
Out[2]: 
{0: 'NOTSET',
 10: 'DEBUG',
 20: 'INFO',
 30: 'WARNING',
 40: 'ERROR',
 50: 'CRITICAL',
 'CRITICAL': 50,
 'DEBUG': 10,
 'ERROR': 40,
 'INFO': 20,
 'NOTSET': 0,
 'WARN': 30,
 'WARNING': 30}

In [3]: logging.addLevelName(logging.DEBUG,'POTATO')

In [4]: logging._levelNames
Out[4]: 
{0: 'NOTSET',
 10: 'POTATO',
 20: 'INFO',
 30: 'WARNING',
 40: 'ERROR',
 50: 'CRITICAL',
 'CRITICAL': 50,
 'DEBUG': 10,
 'ERROR': 40,
 'INFO': 20,
 'NOTSET': 0,
 'POTATO': 10,
 'WARN': 30,
 'WARNING': 30}
```
So I poked around and didn't see the new logging levels having much use, and thought it might be better for (long term) to move in a direction of not having custom levels. Thus I decided to basically keep the original behavior given Python 2, and given Python 3, just mimic the same thing happening (albeit it won't be so useful). 

@JensTimmerman if you think it's ok to override this dictionary for python 2, I can make this change.

    if not return_immediately:

I understand why you went with the number of tests with a specific outcome, but I think using `report.rerun` would be a better solution. Your solution will mean a different hash will be generated if the tests run in a different order, or if a test has a different outcome. By using the run number for the individual test we'll be able to rewrite files in the assets directory on subsequent runs, causing less junk to build up. It also means we can avoid passing the additional integer for every `TestResult` object, as we already have the `report` object.

@thequackdaddy I did some playing around and think I figured it out. It looks like the sqlalchemy dialects have a class attribute called `ischema_names` that is a map between the database type and the sqlalchemy type. You can set it to whatever custom type you want. I made the following example. If you do that for the timestamp type in mssql, then odo should appropriately only pick up bits for that.

```
In [1]: import sqlalchemy

In [2]: from sqlalchemy.dialects.sqlite.base import SQLiteDialect

In [3]: class MyInt(sqlalchemy.INTEGER):
   ...:     pass

In [4]: SQLiteDialect.ischema_names['INTEGER'] = MyInt

In [5]: SQLiteDialect.ischema_names['INT'] = MyInt

In [7]: db = sqlalchemy.create_engine('sqlite:///tutorial.db')

In [8]: meta = sqlalchemy.MetaData()

In [9]: meta.reflect(bind=db)

In [10]: users = meta.tables['users']

In [11]: users.columns.values()
Out[11]:
[Column('user_id', MyInt(), table=<users>, primary_key=True, nullable=False),
 Column('name', VARCHAR(length=40), table=<users>),
 Column('age', MyInt(), table=<users>),
 Column('password', VARCHAR(), table=<users>)]
```
